extracted_rules.resource: Right to choose personal curriculum
proof: [§1] But such is manifestly the science of Politics; for it is this that ordains which of the sciences are to exist in states, and what branches of knowledge the different classes of the citizens are to learn, and up to what point
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the science of Politics as the master authority that dictates what specific knowledge different groups of citizens are permitted or required to acquire, subordinating individual educational choice to state necessity.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies broadly to the 'different classes of citizens' within the Greek polis.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to study and practice politics
proof: [§1] Hence the young are not fit to be students of Political Science. For they have no experience of life and conduct, and it is these that supply the premises and subject matter of this branch of philosophy. And moreover they are led by their feelings; so that they will study the subject to no purpose or advantage, since the end of this science is not knowledge but action.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly bars the young from the study of political science, which in the Greek context is the preparation for active citizenship and governance, based on their supposed lack of experience and emotional immaturity.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to an entire age cohort of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Recognition of human-level happiness
proof: [§1] The generality of mankind then show themselves to be utterly slavish, by preferring what is only a life for cattle; but they get a hearing for their view as reasonable because many persons of high position share the feelings of Sardanapallus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle categorizes the life-aims of the 'multitude' or 'generality of mankind' (common/poor people) as 'slavish' and comparable to 'cattle,' effectively denying that their mode of existence meets the criteria for true human happiness.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to the 'multitude,' encompassing a vast majority of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Ability to perform noble actions
proof: [§1] For many noble actions require instruments for their performance, in the shape of friends or wealth or political power; also there are certain external advantages, the lack of which sullies supreme felicity, such as good birth, satisfactory children, and personal beauty: a man of very ugly appearance or low birth, or childless and alone in the world, is not our idea of a happy man
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that certain material 'instruments' like wealth and social standing ('good birth') are prerequisites for performing the noble actions that constitute happiness. Therefore, those lacking these resources are systematically excluded from the 'Supreme Good.'
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This impacts all individuals of low birth or insufficient wealth.

extracted_rules.resource: Participation in noble activities
proof: [§1] We have good reasons therefore for not speaking of an ox or horse or any other animal as being happy, because none of these is able to participate in noble activities.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly excludes non-human animals from the category of 'happiness' because they cannot partake in the civic or 'noble' activities of the state.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all non-human living creatures.

extracted_rules.resource: Capacity for noble acts
proof: [§1] For this cause also children cannot be happy, for they are not old enough to be capable of noble acts; when children are spoken of as happy, it is in compliment to their promise for the future.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Children are legally and socially defined as incapable of 'noble acts' due to their age, which excludes them from the primary resource of the society: the achievement of 'happiness' (eudaimonia) through virtuous action.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all individuals below the age of adulthood.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to choose branches of knowledge

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes Politics as the master science that exercises authority over the state by determining which subjects are taught to which classes of citizens and to what extent, implying the state (through political science) holds the power to restrict or mandate education rather than the individual.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies generally to all classes of citizens within the state, though the specific curriculum may vary by class.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to study and critique political science

extracted_rules.resource: Access to moral and political science
proof: [§24] And it makes no difference whether they are young in years or immature in character: the defect is not a question of time, it is because their life and its various aims are guided by feeling; for to such persons their knowledge is of no use, any more than it is to persons of defective self-restraint.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle equates those of immature character (often a proxy for youth or those deemed habitually irrational) with those who are barred from the benefits of Moral and Political science, effectively excluding them from the 'target audience' of civic leadership training.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to study political science

extracted_rules.resource: Participation in the Life of Politics
proof: [§34] [5] But let us continue from the point where we digressed. ... the one just mentioned, the Life of Politics, and thirdly, the Life of Contemplation. The generality of mankind then show themselves to be utterly slavish, by preferring what is only a life for cattle

extracted_rules.resource: Right to prioritize truth over personal ties

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from unrealistic precision requirements

extracted_rules.resource: Participation in the fortunes of descendants

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle acknowledges a customary belief (which he treats as a rule of reality in his society) that the status or 'bliss' of deceased individuals is still tied to the worldly success or failure of their living kin.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all deceased members of the society who have living descendants or friends.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to state-sponsored moral education

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom to engage in extramarital relations
proof: [§232] Indeed the very names of some directly imply evil, for instance malice, shamelessness, envy, and, of actions, adultery, theft, murder. All these and similar actions and feelings are blamed as being bad in themselves; it is not the excess or deficiency of them that we blame. It is impossible therefore ever to go right in regard to them—one must always be wrong; nor does right or wrong in their case depend on the circumstances, for instance, whether one commits adultery with the right woman, at the right time, and in the right manner; the mere commission of any of them is wrong.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle lists adultery as an action that is 'bad in itself' and 'always wrong' regardless of circumstances, reflecting the societal and legal prohibition in classical Athens where adultery was a punishable offense against the family structure.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule is a general moral and legal prohibition applying to all married individuals and those interacting with them.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive public honor
proof: [§525] Moreover, if he were bad, he would not be worthy of honor, since honor is the prize of virtue, and the tribute that we pay to the good. ... Honor rendered by common people and on trivial grounds he will utterly despise, for this is not what he merits.

extracted_rules.resource: Recognition of prudence in general conduct
proof: [§826] It is not Science, because matters of conduct admit of variation; and not Art, because doing and making are generically different, since making aims at an end distinct from the act of making, whereas in doing the end cannot be other than the act itself: doing well is in itself the end.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle draws a sharp categorical distinction between 'Making' (Art/Techne) and 'Doing' (Prudence/Praxis). Since artisans are defined by 'making' (which has an external end), they are conceptually excluded from the category of 'Prudence', which is the governing virtue of the citizen and statesman.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This categorizes a broad section of the working population (artisans and craftsmen) as distinct from the governing class of 'prudent' men.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to first principles of action
proof: [§829] The first principles of action are the end to which our acts are means; but a man corrupted by a love of pleasure or fear of pain, entirely fails to discern any first principle, and cannot see that he ought to choose and do everything as a means to this end, and for its sake; for vice tends to destroy the sense of principle.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that those 'corrupted by love of pleasure' (the intemperate) lose the cognitive ability to perceive the 'first principles' required for prudence. In a society where prudence is required for political and domestic authority, this constitutes a rule of exclusion based on a psychological/moral state.

extracted_rules.resource: Recognition of prudence and practical wisdom
proof: [§848] A further proof of what has been said is, that although the young may be experts in geometry and mathematics and similar branches of knowledge, we do not consider that a young man can have Prudence. extracted_rules.resource: Capacity for metaphysical and natural philosophy

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a societal rule where adult children are obligated to provide food and care for their parents, treating it as a legalistic 'debt'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all parents within the social structure.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to preferential seating and formal recognition
proof: [§1126] Again, we should pay to all our seniors the honor due to their age, by rising when they enter, offering them a seat, and so on.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Societal etiquette rules grant the elderly specific material and physical honors, such as the right to have others rise in their presence or yield their seats.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all individuals classified as 'seniors' or elders.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to determine mode of office selection
proof: [§1126] Concord is said to prevail in a state, when the citizens agree as to their interests, adopt the same policy, and carry their common resolves into execution... for instance, there is concord in the state when the citizens unanimously decree that the offices of state shall be elective
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies the collective power of the citizen body to determine the fundamental structure of their government, such as making offices elective.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the entire recognized citizen body of a polis.

extracted_rules.resource: Obligation to labor and public burdens

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a social reality where those he deems 'base' (inferior in character/wealth-usage) systematically avoid the taxes and physical duties (liturgies/burdens) expected of citizens.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Refers to a specific segment of the population distinguished by moral/social status.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to payment for products

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle uses the economic relations of craftsmen as a baseline for 'just' proportion in society, confirming that they have a recognized right to be paid for their labor/products based on a common measure (money).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to the class of manual laborers and artisans within the citizen body.

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a specific legal tradition in some states where the recipient of a service or credit (the debtor) has the legal or customary standing to determine the final value of the return, rather than the creditor.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any person entering a voluntary covenant or credit arrangement in specific jurisdictions.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to paternal-specific respect

proof: [§1145] Honor also is due to parents, as it is to the gods, though not indiscriminate honor: one does not owe to one’s father the same honor as to one’s mother... but one owes to one’s father the honor appropriate to a father, and to one’s mother that appropriate to her.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from self-destruction and social flight

Being 'hated for their wickedness' leads to 'fleeing from life' (suicide) and the inability to be alone, suggesting that the status of 'criminal' results in the loss of the resource of life-security and peace of mind through social hatred.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those defined by the society as 'criminals' or 'wicked,' which is a substantial but specific subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive public goodwill and sympathy

extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the decreeing of alliances (specifically with Sparta) as a concrete political action taken by the citizens when they are in concord, indicating this is a recognized right of the citizen group.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution or death

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the virtuous citizen's relationship to the state (country) as one where the individual is expected to 'lay down his life' if the common interest demands it. Their preferences are categorized as symptoms of 'corruption' rather than legitimate human experiences.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific moral sub-class within the population that is excluded from the standard definition of human experience.

extracted_rules.resource: Authority to define objective reality and social standards
proof: [§1272] But we hold that in all such cases the thing really is what it appears to be to the good man. And if this rule is sound, as it is generally held to be, and if the standard of everything is goodness, or the good man, qua good, then the things that seem to him to be pleasures are pleasures, and the things he enjoys are pleasant.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly identifies the 'good man' (spoudaios) as the 'standard of everything.' This grants a specific elite group the concrete power to define what is true, pleasant, and acceptable for the rest of society.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This concerns the virtuous elite, a significant but minority segment of the citizenry often associated with the ruling class.

extracted_rules.resource: Full participation in the 'human' standard of activities
proof: [§1272] Nor need it cause surprise that things disagreeable to the good man should seem pleasant to some men; for mankind is liable to many corruptions and diseases, and the things in question are not really pleasant, but only pleasant to these particular persons, who are in a condition to think them so.
[§1273] The other pleasures are so only in a secondary or some lower degree, like the activities to which they belong.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle categorizes the activities and pleasures of the 'corrupted' or 'diseased' as being of a 'lower degree,' effectively creating a hierarchy of human value where the rights and sensory claims of these groups are subordinated to the standards of the 'robust' or 'perfect' man.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those suffering from physical or moral ailments that place them outside the 'normative' human category.

extracted_rules.rule_category: Denial of legal and social standing
extracted_rules.resource: Right to be recognized as a happy or flourishing being
proof: [§1282] Also anybody can enjoy the pleasures of the body, a slave no less than the noblest of mankind; but no one allows a slave any measure of happiness, any more than a life of his own.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly states that while slaves can experience physical pleasure, they are categorically excluded from 'happiness' (eudaimonia), which in this context implies the civic and moral recognition of a life lived with purpose and virtue.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire class of enslaved people in Greek society, which was a significant and broad portion of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to a life of one's own
proof: [§1282] Also anybody can enjoy the pleasures of the body, a slave no less than the noblest of mankind; but no one allows a slave any measure of happiness, any more than a life of his own.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text equates the exclusion of slaves from happiness with the denial of 'a life of his own,' indicating that a slave's existence is legally and customarily considered an extension of the master rather than an independent life.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes the fundamental legal and social status of all individuals in the category of 'slave'.

extracted_rules.resource: Recognition of judgment and values in public discourse
proof: [§1278] Children imagine that the things they themselves value are actually the best; it is not surprising therefore that, as children and grown men have different standards of value, so also should the worthless and the virtuous.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle uses children as a benchmark for flawed judgment, noting that their standards of value are not recognized as 'best' or 'actual' by the standards of the community/grown men, excluding their values from serious consideration in the definition of the good life.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all children, a large but specific developmental demographic within the society.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to positions of authority and honor
proof: [§1283] But the activity of the politician also is unleisured, and aims at securing something beyond the mere participation in politics—positions of authority and honor, or, if the happiness of the politician himself and of his fellow-citizens, this happiness conceived as something distinct from political activity
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies that the role of the politician is specifically directed toward securing concrete societal resources: positions of authority and public honor.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those holding or seeking political office, a smaller subset of the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to external goods and wealth
proof: [§1293] Both, it may be granted, require the mere necessaries of life, and that in an equal degree (though the politician does as a matter of fact take more trouble about bodily requirements and so forth than the philosopher); for in this respect there may be little difference between them. But for the purpose of their special activities their requirements will differ widely. The liberal man will need wealth in order to do liberal actions, and so indeed will the just man in order to discharge his obligations (since mere intentions are invisible, and even the unjust pretend to wish to act justly); and the brave man will need strength if he is to perform any action displaying his virtue; and the temperate man opportunity for indulgence: otherwise how can he, or the possessor of any other virtue, show that he is virtuous?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes that the life of the 'politician' or those exercising practical moral virtues requires significant 'external equipment' and 'outward equipment' like wealth and physical strength to actually perform the deeds required by their social and political roles, unlike the philosopher who needs less.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific class of active citizens and leaders (politicians, the 'liberal man') rather than the whole population.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from extensive property ownership
proof: [§1293] But the student, so far as the pursuit of his activity is concerned, needs no external apparatus: on the contrary, worldly goods may almost be said to be a hindrance to contemplation; though it is true that, being a man and living in the society of others, he chooses to engage in virtuous action, and so will need external goods to carry on his life as a human being.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that while the philosopher needs basic necessities, they are specifically exempt from the high material requirements (wealth, power) needed by other virtuous citizens because too much property is a hindrance to their specific social/intellectual function.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a very small subset of the population (intellectuals/philosophers).

extracted_rules.rule_category: Denial of legal and social status
extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in social/civic flourishing (happiness)
proof: [§1293] A further confirmation is that the lower animals cannot partake of happiness, because they are completely devoid of the contemplative activity. [...] but none of the other animals possess happiness, because they are entirely incapable of contemplation.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly excludes non-human animals from 'happiness' (eudaimonia), which in a Greek context is the ultimate goal of civic and individual life, based on their lack of intellect.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all non-human living things.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform noble deeds
proof: [§1293] Yet if supreme blessedness is not possible without external goods, it must not be supposed that happiness will demand many or great possessions; for self-sufficiency does not depend on excessive abundance, nor does moral conduct, and it is possible to perform noble deeds even without being ruler of land and sea: one can do virtuous acts with quite moderate resources. This may be clearly observed in experience: private citizens do not seem to be less but more given to doing virtuous actions than princes and potentates.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle clarifies that the lack of high political office ('ruler of land and sea') or extreme wealth does not block a person from the resource of 'virtuous action.' He explicitly grants this resource to those with 'moderate resources' or 'private citizens.'
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the broad middle-class/citizenry of the polis.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform virtuous actions
proof: [§1303] This may be clearly observed in experience: private citizens do not seem to be less but more given to doing virtuous actions than princes and potentates. It is sufficient then if moderate resources are forthcoming; for a life of virtuous activity will be essentially a happy life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly notes that the ability to perform noble or virtuous deeds—which in Greek society was a prerequisite for full social and legal standing—is not reserved for the elite or those with high political power, but is fully accessible to private citizens with moderate means.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the broad class of non-ruling citizens, which constituted the majority of the free population.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to necessary life sustenance
proof: [§1302] But the philosopher being a man will also need external well—being, since man’s nature is not self—sufficient for the activity of contemplation, but he must also have bodily health and a supply of food and other requirements.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle acknowledges that even those dedicated to intellectual life require a baseline of material resources (food, health, external goods) to function within society, establishing a rule of 'moderate supply' as a necessity for this group.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to any student of philosophy, this group is a subset of the broader citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform noble exploits
proof: [§1304] Solon also doubtless gave a good description of happiness, when he said that in his opinion those men were happy who, being moderately equipped with external goods, had performed noble exploits and had lived temperately; for it is possible for a man of but moderate possessions to do what is right.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Citing Solon's laws/precepts, the text affirms that performing 'noble exploits' (which could include military or civic service) is not the exclusive domain of the extremely rich or powerful, but is a rule applicable to those of moderate estate.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This defines a specific economic strata (moderate vs high wealth) within the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from mandatory state discipline
proof: [§1307] hence the nurture and exercises of the young should be regulated by law, since temperance and hardiness will not be painful when they have become habitual.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that the education and physical training of the young should not be left to private choice but must be dictated by the state through law, thereby removing the autonomy of the young (and their families) over their daily conduct.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to an entire age demographic across the citizen body, making it a broad developmental regulation.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from state-enforced habituation
proof: [§1307] Accordingly we shall need laws to regulate the discipline of adults as well, and in fact the whole life of the people generally; for the many are more amenable to compulsion and punishment than to reason and to moral ideals.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that legal compulsion should extend beyond childhood into adulthood to ensure continued virtuous behavior through the threat of punishment.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule encompasses the 'whole life of the people generally,' targeting nearly the entire adult population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside within the state
proof: [§1307] yet he is bound to impose chastisement and penalties on the disobedient and ill-conditioned, and to banish the incorrigible out of the state altogether.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a societal rule where those who cannot be reformed by habit or law are removed from the political community entirely.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While the 'incorrigible' is a behavioral descriptor, it functions as a legal classification for a subset of the population that is permanently excluded.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to issue binding domestic regulations
proof: [§1307] every man lives as he likes, in Cyclops fashion laying down the law for children and for spouse. ... Paternal exhortations and family habits have authority in the household, just as legal enactments and national customs have authority in the state.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a customary power held by the male head of the household to dictate rules for subordinates (women and children), particularly in states where public education is neglected.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all male heads of households, a significant but specific subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from corporal pain
proof: [§1307] the base, whose desires are fixed on pleasure, must be chastised by pain, like a beast of burden. This indeed is the ground for the view that the pains and penalties for transgressors should be such as are most opposed to their favorite pleasures.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle categorizes 'the many' or 'the base' (often associated with lower social/wealth strata) as a group that must be controlled via physical pain rather than reason, justifying the use of painful penalties.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to 'the many' (the mass of mankind), a very large portion of the society described.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to state-regulated upbringing
proof: [§1315] Hence the nurture and exercises of the young should be regulated by law, since temperance and hardiness will not be painful when they have become habitual.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that because young people find it difficult to live temperately by choice, the state must provide a legal framework for their education and physical training to ensure they develop virtuous habits.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all young future citizens within a Greek polis.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from state-enforced moral discipline
proof: [§1316] Accordingly we shall need laws to regulate the discipline of adults as well, and in fact the whole life of the people generally; for the many are more amenable to compulsion and punishment than to reason and to moral ideals.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that laws should not end with childhood but should extend to adults to ensure continued practice of virtue through compulsion and punishment.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule encompasses the entire adult population of the state.

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a societal rule where individuals who cannot be reformed through laws or habituation are permanently removed from the political community.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to a small subset of the population deemed beyond moral repair.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public legal protection within the home
proof: [§1320] in most states such matters have been entirely neglected, and every man lives as he likes, in Cyclops fashion laying down the law For children and for spouse.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle observes that in most contemporary Greek states (except Sparta), the head of the household has absolute rule over his family members, who lack the protection of a public educational system or shared legal oversight within the domestic sphere.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes the status of the majority of people (women and children) in a typical Greek household.

extracted_rules.resource: Power of legal compulsion
proof: [§1319] Now paternal authority has not the power to compel obedience ... whereas law can enjoin virtuous conduct without being invidious.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While a father has authority based on affection and custom, the text notes that he lacks the formal 'compulsory force' (sanctioned by the state) that the law possesses.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all male heads of households in their relation to the state.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to frame laws
proof: [§1324] So presumably a man who wishes to make other people better (whether few or many) by discipline, must endeavor to acquire the science of legislation ... For to mold aright the character of any and every person that presents himself is not a task that can be done by anybody, but only (if at all) by the man with scientific knowledge.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle frames the ability and right to effectively legislate and educate as a specialized science, effectively excluding those without 'scientific knowledge' or the 'science of legislation' from being legitimate sources of public order.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This identifies a specific elite professional/intellectual class as the only ones fit for the task.
